docs.title: How to Play
docs.text:
Bungi has a lot of rules and mechanics now. This is to help keep everything organized and have something we can refer back to.

🛠 = Currently being changed / reworked

docs.title: 🃏 Stacking
docs.text:
• Can only stack up to 2.
    • Can stack up to 4 with BC tower upgrades.
  • Stack ONLY in production zone
  • Unstack & Field Stack effects (see Structures)

docs.title: ⛄ Units & Production
docs.text:
• You can only produce units in the production zone
  • Unit type limit of 2, can be doubled by the BC/DC Unit type towers
  • Units have separate health, power, costs, effects, movement, and targeting

docs.title: 💓 Hearts
docs.text: • Hearts must be MOVED into to be destroyed (annihilating the moving piece)

docs.title: ❌🎮 Lose conditions
docs.text:
• Lose all hearts
  • Lose all units

docs.title: 🎳🎲 Game Phases
docs.text:
• Initial phase no longer exists, first board state and deck is determined before the game starts
  • Active Phase: Can commit actions and commit counter phase actions
  • Counter phase: Actions will come into play at the end of the other person's turn

🌞🏭 Sprites and Resources

  • There are a finite number of resource tiles w/ a finite number of resources.🛠
  • Sprites generate resources off of any resource tile at the start of your turn.
  • Sprites bypass many unit mechanics.
    • Sprites can innately stack to 4.
    • Sprites can unstack and field stack.
    • Sprites have infinite unit-type limit.
  • Sprites are limited in unique ways.
    • They are limited by a unique “sprite limit”.
    • They do not get omni-actions.
    • They do not spawn with a movement action.

docs.title: 🏢 Structures
docs.text:
• Structures are mainly built with essence
  • Construction time is 1 turn
  • Structures provide massively important effects
  • Structures can be upgraded to access new effects
  • Structures have upgrade paths

docs.title: ✨ Essence
docs.text:
• Essence drops from enemy deaths
  • The amount of essence dropped depends on where on the board the enemy is killed
  • The closer they are killed to your heart tiles, the more essence you get, with a maximum of 3
  • You gain essence per stack shaved off a unit *Essence Emporium is in the structures spreadsheet

docs.title: 👓 Special Must-Know Mechanics
docs.text:
• ← Every other mechanic is explained through structures.
  • Worker Efficiency - A resource tower upgrade that gives you more resources
  • Sprite Pylons - A resource tower upgrade that can allow you to produce outside the production zones
  • Unstacking
  • Field Stacking

docs.title: Stacking
docs.text: Structures that interact with the stacking mechanic EX: Field Stacking Tower, Unstacking Tower

docs.title: Zone Creation
docs.text: Units can designate and/or maintain certain tiles to confer effects/modifications to the units that are on it. Some units that include this: Drummer, Powerfly. Some structures: Sprite Pylons.

docs.title: Contents:
docs.text: • Sprite Structure

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
Provides upgrades and effects for resource gain and resource management. It is meant to create discrete steps the player can follow in order to progress in a match.

  1. (start)
  2. "Allows production of sprites. Sprite limit +4.
  3. Allows Sprites to stack to 4, Sprites can also field stack and unstack."
  4. Cost: 0BC
  5. Sprite Efficiency
  6. "Resource tiles give +1 resource for every pair of Sprites.
  7. Sprite limit +2. (Max 6)"
  8. Cost: 1BC
  9. Sprite Pylon
  10. "Sprites that granted resources this turn are now Sprite Pylons.
  11. Sprite Pylons allow unit production in any cardinally adjacent tile.
  12. Sprite Limit +2. (Max 8)"
  13. Cost: 2BC +1E
  14. Advanced Pylons
  15. "Sprite Pylons now allow unit production in any adjacent tile.
  16. Sprite Limit +2 (Max 10)"
  17. Cost: 2BC +1E
  18. Sprite Recall
  19. "Call back all sprites on a tile and gain back +1 BC per pair of sprites.
  20. You can use this once per turn.
  21. Sprite Limit +2 (Max 12)"
  22. Cost: 2BC +1E

docs.title: Design intention
docs.text:
Each upgrade should feel like a substantial boost to your resource generation and provide you with more options in how you are managing your board state and interacting with resources.

Again not sure about this, the structure spreadsheet works well enough

docs.title: Notes or implementation details
docs.text: !- Make your own folder for each unit.

docs.title: Contents:
docs.text: • Bomber

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Limited movement and blows up.

docs.title: Design intention
docs.text: This should be a low cost unit that has a very telegraphed attack pattern. It is easy to counter but must be countered or otherwise suffer a devastating attack.

docs.title: Notes or implementation details
docs.text: Explosive, blows itself up and everything adjacent to it.

docs.title: Contents:
docs.text:
• Blocked Tiles
  • Stat Modifiers
  • Stunned Tiles
  • Zone Creation

docs.title: Blocked Tiles
docs.text: Tiles that don't allow units to move into them. EX: Deployer, Guard

docs.title: Stat Modifiers
docs.text: Effects that increase or decrease units' base stats, usually health power or armor.

docs.title: Stunned Tiles
docs.text: Tiles that don't allow any movement from any occupying units. EX: Hypnosnail

docs.title: Zone Creation
docs.text: Units can designate and/or maintain certain tiles to confer effects/modifications to the units that are on it. Some units that include this: Drummer, Powerfly. Some structures: Sprite Pylons.

docs.title: Contents:
docs.text:
• Material Count
  • Methodical Growth

docs.title: Material count/ resource drain.
docs.text:
A core concept of Bungi is expanding your control over resources and the game bit by bit in order to create better advantage for yourself.

  • Having low resources should hurt.
  • Using resources improperly should have some negative tradeoff that provides windows of opportunity for the opponent to capitalize on.
  • Helps the player intuitively understand the game.
  • Communicates game state at a glance.
  • Provides clear feedback on mistakes and areas to improve.

docs.title: Methodical Growth
docs.text:
A core concept of Bungi is expanding your control over resources and the game bit by bit in order to create better advantage for yourself.

  • Introduces risk management.
  • Investing resources into future resources provides no immediate payoff, providing a window of opportunity for the opponent.
  • Easy to learn.
  • It should be clear what a risky resource investment is and what a safe one is.
  • Can be changed in response to enemy strategy.
  • Taking riskier growth options when the enemy is playing passive, and taking safer growth options if they need to manage spending resources on units.

docs.title: Contents:
docs.text:
• Armor
  • Bonus Damage
  • Unit Production
  • Unit Type Limits

docs.title: Armor
docs.text: Armor is two parts, armor value and armor stacks. Armor is a specific value that reduces damage by a flat amount. Each time damage is reduced by armor that armor is "used up". And stacks are shaven from the unit's armor stacks.

docs.title: Bonus Damage
docs.text: A mechanic aimed to introduce a very small amount of randomness to break up stalemate situations. Some units have a non-integer amount of power. The non-integer portion is converted into a probability of the chance to do 1 extra damage. For example: 37.25 Power would mean you hit from 37 damage and you have a 0.25% chance to hit for an extra 1pt of damage.

docs.title: Unit Type Limits
docs.text: Units have a maximum for each type of unit. Going over the maximum summons requires burnout summons.

docs.title: Active Phase
docs.text: Active phase

docs.title: Contents:
docs.text:
• Stack Shaving
  • Stacking

docs.title: Stack Shaving
docs.text:
Stacked units have quantized pools of health according to their stack level.

For example: A unit has 2 health. A four stack (4stk) of this unit would have 8 health.

You can then visualise this unit as having 4 distinct health pools of 2hp each.

When a unit takes enough damage such that they would have sustained damage equal to 1x their base stat HP (i.e. 2hp),\ then that unit will now lose one of their stacks.\ Put more simply, whenever they lose enough health that they would deplete a pool of HP, they lose that stack.

So to illustrate:

A 4stk unit has 8hp. * It takes 4dmg. * It is now a 2stk unit with 4hp.

A 4stk unit has 8hp. * It takes 3dmg. * It is now a 3stk unit with 5hp.

Currently calculating the stacks lost will be done with this function. 

\begin{aligned}
&\text{int } \text{stacksLost} = 0; \\
&\text{if (healthLost > baseHealthValue)} \\
&\quad \text{stacksLost} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{healthLost}}{\text{baseHealthValue}} \right\rfloor; \\
&\text{if ((currentHealth - healthLost) \% \text{baseHealthValue} == 0)} \\
&\quad \text{stacksLost} += 1; \\
&\text{return } \text{stacksLost};
\end{aligned}

docs.title: Stacking
docs.text:
Units can be stacked to increase all their stats by 1x their base stat.

All stats are increased additively except for these stats which are unaffected by stacking: * Armor value * Armor shaving * Armor penetration

docs.title: Resolution Time
docs.text:
These times determine when certain actions occur or are resolved.\ Matches the ResolutionTime enum in code.

  • PerFrame
  • AnyUpdate
  • StartOfTurn
  • StartOfActivePhase
  • StartOfCounterPhase
  • StartOfProduction
  • BeforeMovement
  • StartOfStacking
  • StartOfUnstack
  • AfterMovement
  • AfterProducing
  • EndOfTurn
  • EndOfActivePhase
  • EndOfCounterPhase

docs.title: Life Cycle
docs.text:
These lifecycles determine how long an effect lasts.\ Matches the LifeCycle enum in code.

  • Permanent
  • UntilCasterDies
  • AmountOfCounterphases
  • AmountOfApplications
  • AmountOfTurns
